changes_summary: Revised plan addresses all 15 flags: (1) overlap speed formula now uses resolved span from resolvedRows, not requested span; (2) commits to post-processing overlap patches in useClipResize instead of modifying resolveOverlaps; (3) concrete data path via existing useTimelineEditorData() context which already has dataRef; (4) explicit dir destructuring fix and inline type instead of importing unexported ResizeDir; (5) test mock updated with dataRef; (6) visual cue specified concretely with resizeClamped state and ring class; (7) criteria re-prioritized — unautomatable ones demoted, redundant playbackRate criterion removed.
questions: Should resizing a video clip have any speed limits (e.g., min 0.25x, max 4x)? Unconstrained speed could produce very slow or fast playback on extreme resizes.

For left-edge resize on video clips: should this also preserve full source and only change speed (pure speed mode), or is the described behavior correct — trim the start and adjust speed for the remaining portion?

success_criteria.criterion: Video clip right-edge resize changes speed, not from/to. Formula: newSpeed = sourceDuration / timelineDuration
Video clip left-edge resize adjusts from and recalculates speed to preserve source end point
Audio clip resize never modifies speed
Hold-type clips (images/text) are unaffected by this change
Existing TimelineCanvas.test.tsx tests pass (with updated mock including dataRef)
rowsToConfig serialization produces correct from/to/speed after speed-based resize (verified by read-only inspection)
resolveOverlaps is NOT modified — speed conversion happens as post-processing in useClipResize using resolved span from resolvedRows
Post-process overlap patches use resolved action duration from resolvedRows, not the pre-overlap requested span
dir parameter is destructured in onActionResizeEnd callback (not just in type annotation)
Audio clip cannot be extended past source asset duration on the timeline
Audio clamping applies during live drag preview (updateResize), not just on end
Visual feedback (amber ring) shown when audio clip hits its max resize extent
No new files created — all changes in existing files
Manual smoke tests (7 scenarios in Step 8) pass

assumptions: Compounding speed: subsequent resizes work from the clip's current speed/from/to state, not original source.
No speed limits enforced: the user can make a clip arbitrarily slow or fast via resize.
Left-edge video resize = trim start + adjust speed: the left edge changes the source trim point (from), then speed adjusts so the remaining source fills the timeline duration.
Audio left-edge resize: current behavior preserved (adjusts from), with clamping to prevent trimming before source start.
multi-drag does not resize clips — only repositions, so no speed changes needed.
The amber ring visual cue for audio clamp uses existing Tailwind classes, no new CSS file needed.
